name: CeO2 - O
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: To obtain CeO2–O and CeO2-R, 3.47 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.03 g Na3PO4·12H2O (A.R, Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), as well as 1.39 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and 15.36 g NaOH(A.R, National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), were separately dissolved in 64 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 1 h and 30 min, respectively. Subsequent to this, both mixtures were transferred to an autoclave and subjected to the hydrothermal processes at 160 °C and 100 °C for 24 h, respectively. The obtained powder was then calcined in a muffle furnace under the condition of air atmosphere and heating rate of 1 °C/min until reaching 500 °C for 4 h.

name: CeO2 - C
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The CeO2 nano-cube (CeO2–C) was prepared using the hydrothermal method according the procedure reported by Cao et al. [47]. The specific procedure involved dissolving 3.47 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O in 20 mL of ultrapure water, while 13.71 g NaOH was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water. Subsequently, the obtained NaOH solution was added dropwise to above cerium nitrate solution under vigorous stirring. After 30 min of vigorous stirring, the mixed solution was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. obtained powder was then calcined in a muffle furnace under the condition of air atmosphere and heating rate of 1 °C/min until reaching 500 °C for 4 h.

name: CeO2 - P
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The CeO2 powder (CeO2–P) was synthesized following the procedure reported by Zheng et al. [48]. In this method, 2.17 g Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 4 g urea (A.R, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.), and 9.8 g NaOH were combined in 40 mL of ultrapure water and vigorously stirred for 25 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred to an oven at 120 °C for 24 h hydrothermal reaction. The obtained powder was then calcined in a muffle furnace under the condition of air atmosphere and heating rate of 1 °C/min until reaching 500 °C for 4 h.

name: CeO2 - S
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: For CeO2–S, 1.99 g of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (A.R, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.) and 0.8 g of PVP (A.R, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.) were dissolved in a specific amount of ethylene glycol (A.R, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.). Subsequently, 8 mL of ultrapure water was added, and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Following this, the concoction underwent hydrothermal reaction in an autoclave at 160 °C for 24 h and the obtained powder was then calcined in a muffle furnace under the condition of air atmosphere and heating rate of 1 °C/min until reaching 500 °C for 4 h.

flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)

name: Co3O4
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The hierarchical flower-like silver modified Co3O4 nanosheets catalysts were synthesized by solvothermal method [15], [26]. Typically, Co(NO3)2·6H2O and the certain amount of AgNO3 were added into anhydrous methanol and vigorously stirred to obtain purple solution, and the total amount of cobalt and silver atoms is 15 mmol. Subsequently, 1.801 g of CO(NH2)2 was added into the mixed solution and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was treated in an electronic oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The produced purple powder was centrifuged and washed more than 6 times by deionized water and ethanol until that the pH of supernatant is equal to 7. The product was dried in electronic oven at 70 °C overnight, and then calcined at 450 °C for 3 h under the ramping of 1 °C∙min−1. The samples were named as AgxCo (x = 0.02 and 0.1), and x is the atomic ratio calculated by Ag/(Ag + Co). The hierarchical flower-like Co3O4 was synthesized by above methods except the presence of AgNO3.

flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)

name: CuCe0.7Zr0.3
synthesis_para: A series of CuO-CexZr1-xOy catalysts were prepared by a novel surfactant assisted grinding method. In a typical procedure, appropriate amounts of nitrate salts of Cu, Ce and Zr, and oxalic acid (the molar ratio of oxalic acid to metal ions = 1.7:1; the content of CuO was fixed at 10 wt% based on our previous study [28]) were manually ground in an agate mortar for 5 min. Then, an appropriate amount of surfactant PEG 600 was added to the mixture and ground for further 25 min. After that, all the components became a uniform, viscous and muddy precursor. Then, it was dried at 65 ◦C for 24 h, calcined in air at 500 ◦C for 4 h with a heating rate of 5 ◦C/min to obtain the CuO-CexZr1-xOy catalysts. The molar contents of Ce in the CexZr1-xOy were x = 1, 0.7, 0.5, 0.3 and 0, and the corresponding catalysts were denoted as CuCe, CuCe0.7Zr0.3, CuCe0.5Zr0.5, CuCe0.3Zr0.7 and CuZr, respectively.

name: Co3O4
synthesis_para: Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (582 mg or 2.0 mmol) and a certain amount of cerium nitrate hexahydrate were dissolved in 20 mL methanol. The clear solution was gradually poured in 100 mL methanol solution containing 0.656 mg (8.0 mmol) 2-methylimidazole under stirring for 5 min. The precipitated precursor was collected after 24 h by centrifugation. The collected precipitate was thoroughly washed with methanol three times before being dried in an oven at 80 °C. Prepared samples with different molar ratio of Co and Ce (1:0.1, 1:0.2, 1:0.3, 1:0.4, 1:0.5 and 1:0) were marked as Ce@ZIF(1), Ce@ZIF(2), Ce@ZIF(3), Ce@ZIF(4) Ce@ZIF(5) and ZIF-67, respectively. The prepared samples, as described in the previous section, were heated at 300 °C for 3 h. Temperature was slowly ramped with 1 °C/min in static air. Final Co-Ce products with different molar ratios (1:0.1, 1:0.2, 1:0.3, 1:0.4, 1:0.5 and 1:0) were denoted as CoCe-1, CoCe-2, CoCe-3, CoCe-4, CoCe-5 and Co3O4, respectively.

name: COP-CoCe-2
synthesis_para: A reference catalyst was synthesized by co–precipitation method, denoted as COP-CoCe-x, for comparison; 582 mg (2.0 mmol) of cobalt nitrate hexahydrate and a certain amount of cerium nitrate hexahydrate were dissolved in 40 mL distilled water. Subsequently, 40 mL of 0.5 M NaOH solution was added dropwise under vigorous mixing for 30 min. The prepared metal salt solution was transferred to 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, sealed and placed in an oven at 120 °C for 24 h. The product was washed by distilled water and ethanol three times. After drying at 80 °C, according to the TGA profile, Figure S1 (Supplementary Material), the powder was finally calcined at 300 °C for 3 h.

name: CuMn2RC
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: Each and every catalyst was synthesized by the Co-precipitation procedure. All the chemicals use for research work was an analytical reagent grade. A solution of Mn(CH3COO)2.4H2O was added into Cu(NO3)2·3H2O solutions and mixed for 1h. The mixed solution was taken in the burette and added drop-wise to a solution of KMnO4 in vigorous stirring conditions for the Co-precipitation processes. The molar ratio of Cu/Mn in the CuMnOx catalyst was 1:2. The precipitate was filtered and washed several times with hot distilled water to remove all the anions. The loading of (0.25–1.0 wt %) Au in the form of gold chloride (0.05 MHAuCl4.3H2O) solutions was applied by the deposition-precipitation method, to a various CuMnOx catalysts surfaces to improve their performances for CO oxidation. The precipitate obtained was heated at temperature 110 °C for 24h into an oven and calcined at 300 °C for 2h. The RC of precursors was done by the application of CO–Air mixture (4.5% CO) over the hot precursors. The classification of resultant catalysts thus produced was known by the capital letter of precursors use and their suffixes ‘SA’, ‘FA’ and ‘RC’ indicate whether there were obtained by calcinations in air, flowing air or by RC respectively

The molar ratio of Cu/Mn in the CuMnOx catalyst was 1:2. The precipitate was filtered and washed several times with hot distilled water to remove all the anions. The loading of (0.25–1.0 wt %) Au in the form of gold chloride (0.05 MHAuCl4.3H2O) solutions was applied by the deposition-precipitation method, to a various CuMnOx catalysts surfaces to improve their performances for CO oxidation. The precipitate obtained was heated at temperature 110 °C for 24h into an oven and calcined at 300 °C for 2h. The RC of precursors was done by the application of CO–Air mixture (4.5% CO) over the hot precursors. The classification of resultant catalysts thus produced was known by the capital letter of precursors use and their suffixes ‘SA’, ‘FA’ and ‘RC’ indicate whether there were obtained by calcinations in air, flowing air or by RC respectively

name: α-Mn2O3-550C
synthesis_para: α-Mn2O3 was synthesized according to a previously reported procedure [2], corresponding to the direct thermal decomposition of MnCO3 powders in static air. Controlled calcination of MnCO3 powders were carried out at 700 °C for 5 h with a ramping rate of 1 °C·min-1.

name: RT-CeO2
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: The chemicals, cerium nitrate hexahydrate, Ce(NO3)3·6H2Oand ammonia were purchased from Aldrich (Germany, purity 99.99%). CeO2 nanoparticles were prepared by a process that involves the homogeneous precipitation from cerium salt solution with dilute aqueous ammonia. In the case of ceria synthesised at room temperature, the requisite quantity of precursor was dissolved in double-distilled water and was precipitated by dropwise addition of the solution to aqueous ammonia over a period. Excess precipitant was maintained during the process. The obtained light yellow coloured slurry was washed 10 times with deionized water to remove any water-soluble species and subsequently washed 3 times with ethanol to remove water and disrupt hydrogen bonded networks between water and the precursor, which otherwise led to severe aggregation upon heating. The collected precipitate was oven-dried at 110 ​°C for 12 ​h, crushed using an agate mortar, and subsequently calcined at 500 ​°C for 3 ​h in an air atmosphere to obtain the oxide material (RT-Ce)

name: HT-CeO2
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The chemicals, cerium nitrate hexahydrate, Ce(NO3)3·6H2Oand ammonia were purchased from Aldrich (Germany, purity 99.99%). CeO2 nanoparticles were prepared by a process that involves the homogeneous precipitation from cerium salt solution with dilute aqueous ammonia. In the case of ceria synthesised at room temperature, the requisite quantity of precursor was dissolved in double-distilled water and was precipitated by dropwise addition of the solution to aqueous ammonia over a period. Excess precipitant was maintained during the process. The obtained light yellow coloured slurry was washed 10 times with deionized water to remove any water-soluble species and subsequently washed 3 times with ethanol to remove water and disrupt hydrogen bonded networks between water and the precursor, which otherwise led to severe aggregation upon heating. The collected precipitate obtained after ammonia addition to the precursor solution was immediately transferred into a tightly sealed stainless steel vessel and kept in an air oven maintained at 130 ​°C for 24 ​h. The solid collected was dried at 110 ​°C in the air oven and calcined at 500 ​°C for 3 ​h to yield the oxide (HT-Ce).

name: Bulk-NC
synthesis_para: In a typical preparation, the corresponding nitrate salts were dissolved in deionized water to obtain an aqueous solution of Ni2+ and Cu2+ with the expected stoichiometry. The molar ratio of citric acid to total metal ions was 1.1 : 1.0. The resulting solution was evaporated to dryness at 80 ​°C under vigorous stirring. The clear solution was gradually turned into a sol and finally transformed into a gel. Finally, the wet gel was dried at 110 ​°C for 12 ​h and calcined at 550 ​°C for 4 ​h. It should be mentioned that all of the chemicals were of analytical grade (Beijing Chemical Reagents Company) and used without further treatments.

name: NCP
synthesis_para: The 3DOM nickel−copper mixed oxides were prepared by a colloidal crystal template method using PMMA microspheres as hard template and/or P123 (MWaver ​= ​5800 ​g/mol) as soft template. The PMMA microspheres were synthesized according to the procedures described previously [11,24]. Briefly, 115 ​mL of methyl methacrylate (MMA) was added to 1300 ​mL of deionized water in a nitrogen flow (100 ​mL/min), and then an aqueous solution of potassium persulfate (0.4 ​g of K2S2O8 dissolved in 50 ​mL of deionized water) was added rapidly. The resulting suspension was centrifuged to remove the inorganic salt ions, and the washed cake was then placed in an oven at 80 ​°C overnight. For the catalyst preparation, appropriate amounts of (A) Cu(NO3)2⋅3H2O and (B) Ni(NO3)2⋅6H2O (A ​+ ​B ​= ​10 ​mmol and NiO/CuO molar ratio ​= ​3 : 3, 3 : 2, and 3 : 1, respectively) with/without 1.00 ​g of P123 were dissolved in 9 ​mL of an anhydrous EG and MeOH mixed solution (EG/MeOH volumetric ratio ​= ​1 : 2, 1 : 1.5, and 1 : 1, respectively) under stirring at room temperature (RT) for 3 ​h to obtain a transparent solution. 2.00 ​g of PMMA microspheres was soaked in the as-prepared solution, and the excessive liquid was filtered via a Buchner funnel connected to a vacuum (0.07 ​MPa). After that, the obtained wet PMMA template was dried in an air atmosphere at RT for 12 ​h. The dried sample was then transferred to a ceramic boat that was placed in a tubular furnace and annealed in an air atmosphere at a ramp of 1 ​°C/min from RT to 300 ​°C for 3 ​h. After calcination, the sample was cooled to RT in the same atmosphere and then purged in an air flow of 50 ​mL/min. The solid was heated from RT to a desired temperature (450, 550 or 650 ​°C) in the same atmosphere at a ramp of 1 ​°C/min and maintained at this temperature for 4 ​h. As shown in Table 1 and S1, (i) the sample derived with a NiO: CuO molar ratio of 3 : 3, EG/MeOH volumetric ratio of 1 : 2 in the presence of PMMA and after calcination at 550 ​°C was denoted as NCP;

name: CuMn 2SA
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: All the catalysts were synthesized by the co-precipitation method. The chemicals used for research work were of analytical reagent grade. A solution of Mn(CH3COO)2.4H2O was added to (X = Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2.3H2O/Cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3)/Cobalt nitrate Co(NO3)2.6H2O/Iron nitrate (Fe(NO3)3 9H2O) and stirred for 1 h. The mixed solution was taken in the burette and added drop-wise to a solution of KMnO4 under vigorous stirring conditions for co-precipitation purposes. The resultant precipitate was stirred continuously for 2 h. The molar ratio of (X : Mn) into the catalyst was (1:2). The precipitate was vacuum filtered and washed several times with (30% Conc. HCL) acid and hot distilled water to remove all the anions. The cake thus obtained was dried at temperature 110 °C for 24 h into an oven and calcined at 300 °C for 2 h. The amount of precursor taken for single batch of synthesis is 5gm. All the precursors were calcined in stagnant air at 300 °C just above the decomposition temperatures of precursors for 2 h in a muffle furnace.

name: FeMn2RC
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: All the catalysts were synthesized by the co-precipitation method. The chemicals used for research work were of analytical reagent grade. A solution of Mn(CH3COO)2.4H2O was added to (X = Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2.3H2O/Cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3)/Cobalt nitrate Co(NO3)2.6H2O/Iron nitrate (Fe(NO3)3 9H2O) and stirred for 1 h. The mixed solution was taken in the burette and added drop-wise to a solution of KMnO4 under vigorous stirring conditions for co-precipitation purposes. The resultant precipitate was stirred continuously for 2 h. The molar ratio of (X : Mn) into the catalyst was (1:2). The precipitate was vacuum filtered and washed several times with (30% Conc. HCL) acid and hot distilled water to remove all the anions. The cake thus obtained was dried at temperature 110 °C for 24 h into an oven and calcined at 300 °C for 2 h. The amount of precursor taken for single batch of synthesis is 5gm. The calcinations were carried out by the introduction of low concentration of chemically reactive CO–Air mixture (4.6% CO) at a total flow rate of 32.5 ml min over the hot precursors. The temperature of bed was increased from room temperature to 160 °C where CO conversion has started. This temperature was maintained for a definite period of time and CO concentration was measured in the exit stream of reactor at regular intervals until 100% CO conversion was achieved. After achieving the total CO oxidation the resultant catalyst was annealed for half an hour at the same temperature then the temperature was increased up to 300 °C and upheld for an hour followed by cooling to room temperature in the same environment.

synthesis_para: All the catalysts were synthesized by the co-precipitation method. The chemicals used for research work were of analytical reagent grade. A solution of Mn(CH3COO)2.4H2O was added to (X = Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2.3H2O/Cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3)/Cobalt nitrate Co(NO3)2.6H2O/Iron nitrate (Fe(NO3)3 9H2O) and stirred for 1 h. The mixed solution was taken in the burette and added drop-wise to a solution of KMnO4 under vigorous stirring conditions for co-precipitation purposes. The resultant precipitate was stirred continuously for 2 h. The molar ratio of (X : Mn) into the catalyst was (1:2). The precipitate was vacuum filtered and washed several times with (30% Conc. HCL) acid and hot distilled water to remove all the anions. The cake thus obtained was dried at temperature 110 °C for 24 h into an oven and calcined at 300 °C for 2 h. The amount of precursor taken for single batch of synthesis is 5gm. The calcinations were carried out by the introduction of low concentration of chemically reactive CO–Air mixture (4.6% CO) at a total flow rate of 32.5 ml min over the hot precursors. The temperature of bed was increased from room temperature to 160 °C where CO conversion has started. This temperature was maintained for a definite period of time and CO concentration was measured in the exit stream of reactor at regular intervals until 100% CO conversion was achieved. After achieving the total CO oxidation the resultant catalyst was annealed for half an hour at the same temperature then the temperature was increased up to 300 °C and upheld for an hour followed by cooling to room temperature in the same environment.

name: 30 wt% CuO.Mn2O3
synthesis_para: The xCuO⋅Mn2O3 (the Cu content (x) = 10, 20, 30 wt%) catalysts were fabricated using a colloidal crystal template method. The PMMA microspheres were synthesized using the method described in the Supplementary material. In a typical synthesis, appropriate amounts of Cu(NO3)2⋅3H2O and Mn(NO3)2⋅4H2O and 1.00 g of P123 (MWaver. = 5800 g/mol) were dissolved in 9 mL of an anhydrous ethylene glycol (EG) and methanol (MeOH) mixed solution (EG/MeOH volumetric ratio = 1/1) under stirring at ambient temperature for 3 h to obtain a transparent solution, in which the molar ratio of metals/P123 was 232. After that, 2.00 g of the PMMA was added into the above obtained mixture solution. After complete soaking of the PMMA microspheres for 3 h, the excess solution was removed via a Buchner funnel connected to a vacuum. Then, the prepared wet PMMA template was dried in air at ambient temperature for 12 h, and the dried sample was put in a ceramic boat in a tubular furnace for calcination at a ramp rate of 1 °C/min in N2 atmosphere at 300 °C for 3 h. After calcination, the sample was cooled to ambient temperature and purged with an air stream (50 mL/min), and the obtained solid was heated to 350 °C and maintained at this temperature for 4 h to convert the partially solidified metal precursors to the xCuO⋅Mn2O3 catalysts.

name: P-Co3O4
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: Pristine Co3O4 (named as P-Co3O4) was synthesized as follows. In a typical procedure, 5 g of urea and 0.84 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in deionized water, and stirred for 10-minute. Then the mixture was dried for 12 h to evaporate water at 60 °C to form magenta Co(OH)2 precipitates. Finally, the precipitates were collected and placed into a crucible without a cover, followed by a thermal annealing at 550 °C for 2 h in the muffle furnace with a rate of 15 °C min−1

name: AgCo
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: Composite oxide containing silver and cobalt (1:1 molar ratio) catalysts were prepared by the coprecipitation method. AgNO3 (Aldrich, 99.8%) and Co(NO3)2.6H2O (Fluka, 99.0%) at desired molar ratios were dissolved in distilled water. The total concentration of the metals in the combined aqueous solution was 0.1 M. A sodium carbonate solution (1 M) was added to this solution with stirring at a rate of 2–3 ml/min. The precipitate was aged at its pH of 8 for 3 h and then filtered and washed with hot distilled water several times for removing excess ions. The precipitate was then dried in air at 110°C overnight. After drying, catalysts were calcined in air either at 200°C or at 500°C for 3 h. Cobalt oxide and silver oxide catalysts were prepared with the same procedure for comparison.

name: FC-400
synthesis_para: Mixed oxides containing iron and cobalt catalysts were prepared by co-precipitation method. In a typical preparation, firstly, Fe(NO3)3.9H2O (Merck, 98%) and Co(NO3)2.6H2O (Merck, 99%) with molar ratio of Fe/Co=1/1 were dissolved in distillated water and the resulting solution was heated to 70 °C and an aqueous solution of sodium carbonate (0.5 M) was added to mixed nitrate solution under continuous stirring at a rate of 2–3 ml/min at 70 °C until pH 7.0 was achieved. The resulting precipitate was aged at pH of 7 for 2 h and then filtered and washed with warm distilled water several times for removing excess ions. The precipitate was then dried in air at 110 °C for 16 h. The as-prepared catalysts were calcined at 400, 500 and 600 °C in air atmosphere for 6 h with a ramp rate of 3 °C/min. These samples are denoted with FC-400, FC-500 and FC-600, respectively.

name: Cu1Mn2-550
synthesis_para: Spinel oxide-type CuxMn3-xO4 catalysts were directly synthesized by USP method. In a typical USP process, three steps including the atomization of precursor, the transportation of aerosol and the collection of final product powder are involved, as schematically described in Fig. 1. The aqueous solution of copper nitrate and manganese nitrate (AR, 99.7%, Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. China) without further purification are used as the precursors of Cu/Mn ions. The atomic ratios of Cu:Mn were designed as 1:0, 1:1, 1:2, 2:1 and 0:1, respectively, to investigate the composition effect. The total concentration of Cu2+ and Mn2+ was maintained at 0.18 M. After stirring the mixed solution for 30 min, the precursors were atomized by a domestic ultrasonic nebulizer (1.7 MHz) to form aerosol droplets, which were subsequently transported into the quartz tube (40 mm in dia., 160 cm in length) in furnace heated at 550°C. The catalyst particles deposited on the filter in the outlet of the tube furnace were collected and further annealed at 300 °C for 2 h for series of characterizations. The catalysts are labeled as CuXMnY-Z, where X, Y represent the ratio of Cu2+ and Mn2+ in initial precursors and Z denotes the synthesis temperature Z °C.

name: CeO2
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: The different mixed oxide catalysts were prepared by slightly modified two-steps solution combustion synthesis using Ce(NO3)3.6H2O and Cu(NO3)2.3H2O as oxidizers and citric acid (C6H8O7) as a fuel [23]. For the synthesis of CeO2, CuO and CexCux−1O2 mixed oxides, appropriate volumes of 0.5 M stock solutions of cerium nitrate, copper nitrate and citric acid, all dissolved in ultrapure deionized water, were mixed together. For the synthesis of CexCu1−xO2−δ, a suitable amount of copper nitrate was used to obtain catalysts with the general formula CexCu1−xO2−δ, where x = 0, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 1. For all catalysts, the amount of citric acid was chosen so that the mole ratio of the citric acid to the total metal ions is 1.5:1 (slight excess of citric acid). The reaction mixture of the metal nitrate precursors and the fuel were heated under stirring in a beaker placed in a sand-bath over a hotplate at 90 °C. The reaction mixture was left under heating and stirring till evaporation of excess water and formation of gel-like material. The gel-like material formed was then transferred to a muffle furnace pre-heated to 380 °C and was kept at this temperature for 4 h leading to combustion and formation of spongy solid product. The spongy solid product was calcined in static air at 550 °C for 4 h with heating rate of 5 °C/min. Finally, the resulting solid was ground to fine powder and used as is for characterization and catalytic testing.

The reaction mixture of the metal nitrate precursors and the fuel were heated under stirring in a beaker placed in a sand-bath over a hotplate at 90 °C. The reaction mixture was left under heating and stirring till evaporation of excess water and formation of gel-like material. The gel-like material formed was then transferred to a muffle furnace pre-heated to 380 °C and was kept at this temperature for 4 h leading to combustion and formation of spongy solid product. The spongy solid product was calcined in static air at 550 °C for 4 h with heating rate of 5 °C/min. Finally, the resulting solid was ground to fine powder and used as is for characterization and catalytic testing.

name: CuO.Fe2O3
synthesis_para: The catalysts were synthesized using a solid-state method. In this method, depending on the composition of the mixed oxide, the calculated amounts of Cu and Fe salt precursors (Cu(NO3)2⋅3H2O, Fe(NO3)3⋅9H2O) were mixed with appropriate content of ammonium carbonate for 20 min using a mortar and pestle. In this step, the hydroxyl groups of the metal salt precursors were released and the release of these hydroxyl groups converted the mixture to a pasty form. The resultant mixture was then heated with a rate of 3 °C/min at 400 °C for 2 h.

name: CPS
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: The optimized Ce0.80Hf0.12Sn0.08O2−δ (CHS), Ce0.80Zr0.12Sn0.08O2−δ (CZS), Ce0.80Pr0.12Sn0.08O2−δ (CPS), and Ce0.80La0.12Sn0.08O2−δ (CLS) (mol ratio based on oxides) ternary oxide solid solutions were synthesized by a coprecipitation method. In detail, the desired quantities (see Table S1, ESI†) of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, SnCl4·5H2O, and other respective metal precursors such as HfCl4, ZrO(NO3)2·xH2O, Pr(NO3)3·6H2O, and La(NO3)3·6H2O were dissolved in distilled water separately and then mixed together for 1 h. Under constant stirring, aqueous ammonia solution was added dropwise to the mixture until the precipitation was complete, at which point the pH was approximately 9.0. The resulting suspensions were stirred for another 12 h, aged for 24 h and then vacuum filtered. Afterwards, the precipitates were washed thoroughly with distilled water and the obtained solids were dried at 373 K overnight and finally calcined at 773 K for 5 h. For comparison, the same synthesis route was employed for the preparation of CeO2.

name: Cr3Co16Ox
synthesis_para: A series of binary M3Co16Ox oxide catalysts were prepared by the solid-phase method as reported in our previous work [33]. In a typical synthesis, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (≥99.0%), Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (≥99.0%), oxalic acid (OA, ≥99.0%) and ethlyeneglycol (EG, ≥99.0%) at calculated ratios were measured and ground in an agate mortar for 30 min. Then the mixed solution was transferred from the agate mortar into a glass cup. After being ultrasonicated for 1 h, the sample was dried at 110 °C overnight and then calcined at 230 °C for 4 h. The catalyst was denoted as Cr3Co16Ox. Other M3Co16Ox (M = Ti, Zr, Fe, Mn) oxide catalysts (Co/M = 16:3, EG/OA = 1:1, mol) were prepared through the same procedure.
description: mesoporous materials coated by polymer nanofilm

name: Mn3Co16Ox-free
synthesis_para: The Mn3Co16Ox catalyst without coating polymer was prepared as follows. The Mn3Co16Ox catalyst (2 g) was dissolved in dichloromethane (10 mL) in a 20 mL vial, followed by the addition of trifluoroacetic acid (2 mL). After stirring at room temperature for 36 h, the solvent was removed by vacuum. The sample was then dissolved in 10 mL of THF and precipitated in the mixed solution of hexane and ethlyeneglycol four times. After drying the sample in vacuum at 40 °C, it was calcined at 230 °C for 4 h. The catalyst was denoted as Mn3Co16Ox-free.

name: CuSA1
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The CuMnOx catalysts were prepared by the Co-precipitation method. A solution of Mn(CH3COO)2.4H2O was added to copper(II) nitrate (Cu(NO3)2.3H2O) and stirred for 1 h. The mixed solution was taken in the burette and added drop-wise to a solution of KMnO4 under strong stirring conditions for co-precipitation purposes. The resultant precipitate was stirred continuously for 2 h. In the previous research, the optimum molar ratio of (Cu:Mn) into the CuMnOx catalyst was (1:8.3). The precipitate was filtered and washed several times with hot distilled water to remove all the anions. The precipitate thus obtained was dried at temperature 110 °C for 24 h in an oven and calcined in stagnant air at 300 °C just above the decomposition temperature of the precursors for 2 h in a muffle furnace.

name: MnSA1
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The manganese oxide (MnOx) catalysts were prepared from the manganese acetate salt Mn(CH3COO)2.4H2O by precipitation of drop-wise addition of KMnO4 solution. Copper oxide (CuOx) catalysts were prepared from the copper nitrate Cu(NO3)2.3H2O salt by precipitation of drop-wise addition of KMnO4 solution. The CuMnOx catalysts were prepared by the Co-precipitation method. A solution of Mn(CH3COO)2.4H2O was added to copper(II) nitrate (Cu(NO3)2.3H2O) and stirred for 1 h. The mixed solution was taken in the burette and added drop-wise to a solution of KMnO4 under strong stirring conditions for co-precipitation purposes. The resultant precipitate was stirred continuously for 2 h. In the previous research, the optimum molar ratio of (Cu:Mn) into the CuMnOx catalyst was (1:8.3). The precipitate was filtered and washed several times with hot distilled water to remove all the anions. The precipitate thus obtained was dried at temperature 110 °C for 24 h in an oven and calcined in stagnant air at 300 °C just above the decomposition temperature of the precursors for 2 h in a muffle furnace.

flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The manganese oxide (MnOx) catalysts were prepared from the manganese acetate salt Mn(CH3COO)2.4H2O by precipitation of drop-wise addition of KMnO4 solution. name: CuRC1
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The CuMnOx catalysts were prepared by the Co-precipitation method. A solution of Mn(CH3COO)2.4H2O was added to copper(II) nitrate (Cu(NO3)2.3H2O) and stirred for 1 h. The mixed solution was taken in the burette and added drop-wise to a solution of KMnO4 under strong stirring conditions for co-precipitation purposes. The resultant precipitate was stirred continuously for 2 h. In the previous research, the optimum molar ratio of (Cu:Mn) into the CuMnOx catalyst was (1:8.3). The precipitate was filtered and washed several times with hot distilled water to remove all the anions. The precipitate thus obtained was dried at temperature 110 °C for 24 h in an oven and calcined under in situ reactive calcination (RC) as described. Reactive calcination of the precursors was carried out by the introduction of a low concentration of chemically reactive CO–Air mixture (4.5% CO) at a total flow rate of 32.5 ml min−1 over the hot precursors. The temperature of the reactor bed was increased from room temperature (25 °C) to 160 °C where CO conversion has initiated. This temperature was maintained for a defined period of time. The CO concentration was measured in the exit stream of the reactor at regular intervals until 100% CO conversion was achieved. After achieving total CO conversion the resultant catalyst was annealed for half an hour at the same temperature then the temperature was increased up to 300 °C and upheld for an hour followed by cooling to room temperature in the same environment.

name: MnO2
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: The MnO2, Mn–Cu, and Mn–Cu–M (M = Ce, Co, Cr, and Fe) catalysts were synthesized using the redox precipitation method as follows: 5 g of KMnO4 was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water (DI water). At the same time, 20.25 g of Mn acetate and a calculated amount of a dopant metal precursor were dissolved together in DI water for 1 h. After they had dissolved completely, a KMnO4 solution was slowly added to mixed metal precursor solution under stirring. The solution was stirred for 24 h at room temperature, and brownish precipitant resulted. The precipitated product was filtered and washed several times using DI water and ethanol. The filtered resultant was dried under vacuum at 60 °C. The dried product was calcined in air at 300 °C for 2 h. The samples were denoted as Mn–Cu(x) for binary catalyst, where x represents the nominal molar content of Cu in precursor solution (Mn/Cu = 1:x). The composition of M in Mn–Cu–M ternary catalysts was fixed to 0.05 (Mn/Cu/M = 1.0:0.1:0.05).

The solution was stirred for 24 h at room temperature, and brownish precipitant resulted. The precipitated product was filtered and washed several times using DI water and ethanol. The filtered resultant was dried under vacuum at 60 °C. The dried product was calcined in air at 300 °C for 2 h. The samples were denoted as Mn–Cu(x) for binary catalyst, where x represents the nominal molar content of Cu in precursor solution (Mn/Cu = 1:x). The composition of M in Mn–Cu–M ternary catalysts was fixed to 0.05 (Mn/Cu/M = 1.0:0.1:0.05).

name: CuMnSA
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The CuMnOx catalysts were synthesized by the co-precipitation method. All the materials used for the preparation was analytical grade. A solution of Mn (CH3COO)2·4H2O (14.70 g in 33 mL H2O) was added to 3.68 g of copper (II) nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·2.5H2O) and stirred for 1 h. The mixed solution was taken in the burette and added drop-wise to a solution of KMnO4 (6.32 g in 33 mL H2O) under vigorous stirring conditions for the co-precipitation purpose. The resultant precipitate was stirred continuously for 2 h. The molar ratio of (Cu:Mn) into the CuMnOx catalyst was (1:8.3). The precipitate was filtered and washed several times with hot distilled water to remove all the anions [33]. The doping of (1–5wt%) Co(NO3)2·3H2O by the deposition-precipitation method, to a mixed CuMnOx catalyst to improve their performance for CO oxidation. The obtained cake was dried at temperature 110 °C for 24 h into the oven and calcined in stagnant air at 300 °C above the decomposition temperatures of the precursors for 2 h in a muffle furnace.

name: CuMnCoRC3
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The CuMnOx catalysts were synthesized by the co-precipitation method. All the materials used for the preparation was analytical grade. A solution of Mn (CH3COO)2·4H2O (14.70 g in 33 mL H2O) was added to 3.68 g of copper (II) nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·2.5H2O) and stirred for 1 h. The mixed solution was taken in the burette and added drop-wise to a solution of KMnO4 (6.32 g in 33 mL H2O) under vigorous stirring conditions for the co-precipitation purpose. The resultant precipitate was stirred continuously for 2 h. The molar ratio of (Cu:Mn) into the CuMnOx catalyst was (1:8.3). The precipitate was filtered and washed several times with hot distilled water to remove all the anions [33]. The doping of (1–5wt%) Co(NO3)2·3H2O by the deposition-precipitation method, to a mixed CuMnOx catalyst to improve their performance for CO oxidation. The obtained cake was dried at temperature 110 °C for 24 h in the oven. The reactive calcination of the precursors was carried out in situ in a down flow bench-scale tubular reactor with a defined amount of the precursor diluted with α-alumina to make a total volume of 1 ml at atmospheric pressure. The amount of each precursor taken in the reactor was equivalent to 100 mg weight of the catalyst. The reactor was placed vertically in a split open micro-processor based temperature controlled furnace. The heating rate of the bed was 2 °C min−1 with a temperature control of ±0.5 °C. Reactive calcination of the precursors was carried out by the introduction of a low concentration of chemically reactive CO–Air mixture (4.6% CO) at a total flow rate of 32.5 ml min−1 over the hot precursors. Digital gas flow meters were used to evaluate the flow rates of CO and air to feed the mixture (dried and CO2 free) in the required proportion into the reactor. A thermocouple placed in the thermo-well of the reactor in contact with the precursor bed measured the temperature of the bed. The temperature of the bed was increased from room temperature to 160 °C where CO conversion has started. This temperature was maintained for a defined period of time and the CO concentration was measured in the exit stream of the reactor at regular intervals until 100% CO conversion was achieved. After achieving total CO conversion the resultant catalyst was annealed for half an hour at the same temperature then the temperature was increased up to 300 °C and upheld for an hour followed by cooling to room temperature in the same environment. For the comparative studies the catalysts were also prepared by usual calcination of the various precursors in the air at 300 °C for an hour.

name: CuMnCeSA
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The CuMnCe catalyst was prepared by the co-precipitation method. The aqueous solutions of copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O) at 5.8636 g/100 ml, cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)2·3H2O) at 0.316987 g/100 ml and manganese nitrate (Mn(NO3)2·6H2O) at 10 ml/100 ml were premixed. The resulting solution was stirred and precipitated by an aqueous solution of sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) at 21.2 g/100 ml to maintain pH = 8.3. The precipitates were immediately recovered by filtration, washing several times with hot distilled water and then dried at 120 °C for 24 h and calcined at 415 °C for 2 h. The CuMnCo catalyst was also prepared by the co-precipitation method. The aqueous solutions of copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O) at 5.8636 g/100 ml, cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2·3H2O) at 0.2124 g/100 ml and manganese nitrate (Mn(NO3)2·6H2O) at 10 ml/100 ml were premixed. The resulting solution was stirred and precipitated by an aqueous solution of sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) at 21.2 g/100 ml to maintain pH = 8.3. The formed carbonate precipitate was aged with stirring at room temperature for 8 h. The obtained aged solid was filtered and washed several times with 80 ml ethanol and distilled water several times until neutrality. After the filtering and washing process, the precursor was dried at 120 °C for 24 h and calcined at 415 °C for 2 h (Fig. 1 ).

name: CuMnZrRC
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: A typical procedure for the preparation of (Cu-Ce-Zr) catalysts is as follows. First, analytical grade copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)23H2O), cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)36H2O) and zirconium nitrate (Zr(NO3)45H2O) were blended to form a homogenized premix, in which the molar ratio of (Ce:Zr) was (7:3). Then oxylic acid ((COOH)22H2O), a ligand for the metal cations of Cu2+, Ce3+ and Zr4+ was added to the premix and ground in an agate mortar at room temperature. After mixing, it was placed into a bowl for a reactive grinder, which revolves at a speed of 150 rpm for 4 h. After being ground for 4 h, all of the reactants were transferred to a uniform, viscous and muddy precursor for the CuOCe0.7Zr0.3O2 catalyst. Thereafter the precursor was dried at 120 °C for 24 h and further calcined in air at 415 °C for 2 h at a rate of 5 °C/min. The calcination of the precursor was done just before the activity measurement of the catalyst. It was carried out in three ways; in the first we used the stagnant air calcination (SAC) in the stagnant air, second was flowing air calcination (FAC) in the presence of air and third was reactive calcination (RC) in the presence of 4.5% of CO in air in a compact bench scale of fixed bed tubular flow reactor (Table 1 ).

name: Co3O4
synthesis_para: The catalysts were prepared by a solid-phase method mediated with oxalic acid (OA)/ethlyene glycol (EG). In detail, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (≥98.5%), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (≥99.0%) and C2H2O4·2H2O (OA, ≥99.0%) at designed ratios were weighed and put in an agate mortar at the room temperature. To facilitate the reaction and dispersion of this solid mixture, a measured amount of ethylene glycol was added. After hand grinding for 30min, the sample was transferred to glass cup, ultrasonicated for 1h, then dried at 110°C overnight. Finally the sample was heated from the room temperature to the given temperature (200–400°C) in air at 5°C/min and kept at this temperature for 3h. The prepared catalysts were referred to as FeaCobOx-(OAc-EGd) (Fe/Co=a/b; OA/Co=c/b; EG/Co=d/b, mol).

name: Fe2O3
synthesis_para: Pure Fe2O3, pure CuO and Fe2O3 mixed with different wt.% of CuO (1–50wt.%) were prepared by co-precipitation method [8,14,20]. In a typical preparation, calculated amounts of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (Merck, 99%) and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, 99%) were dissolved in bidistilled water and the resulting solutions were heated to 70°C. Then an aqueous solution of potassium carbonate (0.2M) was added to the mixed nitrate solutions, under continuous stirring, at a rate of 2–3mlmin−1 at 70°C until pH 8.3–8.5 was achieved. The resulting precipitates were aged at this pH for 2h and then filtered and washed with warm bidistilled water several times to remove the excess ions. The precipitates were dried at 110°C overnight before being calcined at 300–600°C in air atmosphere for 3h.

name: Mn1Ce1
synthesis_para: MnOx-CeO2 catalysts with different Mn/Ce mole ratios (0.33–2) were prepared by a redox co-precipitation method [34]. The KMnO4 precursor dissolved in deionized water, in a 10% stoichiometric excess, was titrated with a solution of Ce(NO3)3 and Mn(NO3)2 at 60 °C under vigorous stirring, keeping pH = 8.0 ± 0.3 using a 0.2 M KOH solution. After titration, the solid was digested for 30 min at 60 °C and then filtered, repeatedly washed with hot distilled water, and dried overnight at 100 °C. Aliquots of the dried samples were further calcined in air at 400 °C (6 h).  N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were collected on an Autosorb-6 at −198 °C.

name: LaMnO3
synthesis_para: The well-arrayed colloid crystal template PMMA microspheres with an average diameter of ca. 300 nm (Figure S1, Supporting Information) were synthesized according to the procedures described elsewhere. (32) LaMO3 and MOx (M = Mn and Co) samples were prepared using the PEG-assisted PMMA-templating strategy. In a typical fabrication procedure, stoichiometric amounts of metal nitrates were dissolved in a mixed solution of PEG (MW = 400 g/mol), EG, water, and methanol (MeOH) at room temperature (RT) under stirring for 4 h to obtain a transparent solution (preparation parameters are summarized in Table 1). Then, the PMMA template was added to the transparent solution and soaked for 5 h. After being filtered, the mixture was dried at RT for 48 h; the obtained powders were subsequently heated in N2 (200 mL/min) at 300 °C for 3 h, cooled to 50 °C in the same atmosphere, and finally calcined in air (250 mL/min) at a certain temperature (450–750 °C) for 4 h to remove the template, thus generating the spherical samples. For comparison purposes, the nanosized LaMnO3 and LaCoO3 samples were prepared via the Pechini method (33) and after calcination at 850 and 750 °C, respectively; the nanosized MOx (i.e., Mn2O3 and Co3O4) samples were also prepared via the thermal decomposition method with Mn(NO3)2·4H2O and Co(NO3)2·6H2O as the metal source and after calcination at 700 °C for 5 h.

name: LaCoO3
synthesis_para: The well-arrayed colloid crystal template PMMA microspheres with an average diameter of ca. 300 nm (Figure S1, Supporting Information) were synthesized according to the procedures described elsewhere. (32) LaMO3 and MOx (M = Mn and Co) samples were prepared using the PEG-assisted PMMA-templating strategy. In a typical fabrication procedure, stoichiometric amounts of metal nitrates were dissolved in a mixed solution of PEG (MW = 400 g/mol), EG, water, and methanol (MeOH) at room temperature (RT) under stirring for 4 h to obtain a transparent solution (preparation parameters are summarized in Table 1). Then, the PMMA template was added to the transparent solution and soaked for 5 h. After being filtered, the mixture was dried at RT for 48 h; the obtained powders were subsequently heated in N2 (200 mL/min) at 300 °C for 3 h, cooled to 50 °C in the same atmosphere, and finally calcined in air (250 mL/min) at a certain temperature (450–750 °C) for 4 h to remove the template, thus generating the spherical samples. For comparison purposes, the nanosized LaMnO3 and LaCoO3 samples were prepared via the Pechini method (33) and after calcination at 850 and 750 °C, respectively; the nanosized MOx (i.e., Mn2O3 and Co3O4) samples were also prepared via the thermal decomposition method with Mn(NO3)2·4H2O and Co(NO3)2·6H2O as the metal source and after calcination at 700 °C for 5 h.

name: Fe2O3
synthesis_para: Fe-Cu mixed metal oxide nanopowders with different Cu/Fe molar ratios were synthesized via a simple sol-gel route using propylene oxide as a gelation agent. In a typical synthesis, Fe(NO3)3-9H2O (10 mmol) and a calculated amount of Cu(NO3)2-3H2O were dissolved in an appropriate amount of absolute ethanol at room temperature. After stirring for 15 min, a calculated amount of propylene oxide (propylene oxide/(Fe + Cu) molar ratio = 11) was added to the prepared solution. Gel formation occurred within 5 min. The resulting gel was aged at room temperature for 1 h, dried at 80 °C for 48 h, and calcined at various temperatures, with a ramp rate of 3 °C/min, for 5 h in air. The nominal CuO contents were 10, 15, and 20 mol%, and the corresponding catalysts were denoted by FeCu10, FeCu15, and FeCu20, respectively.

name: NM-LaCoO3-4
synthesis_para: The typical synthesis procedure is as following: 1.3g lanthanum nitrate (La(NO3)3 nH2O) and 1.16g cobaltous nitrate (Co(NO3)2 6H2O) were dissolved in 20mL solvent (ethanol: deionized water=3: 1). Citric acid was added into the nitrate precursors as the chelating agent, and the molar ratio of the citric acid and metal ions was 1:1. The lanthanum and cobaltous composite was placed in oven and calcinated at 270°C for 2h firstly and then at 700°C under atmosphere.. The lanthanum and cobaltous composite was placed in oven and calcinated at 270°C for 2h firstly and then at 700°C under atmosphere.

name: LaCoO3-4
synthesis_para: The as-prepared mesoporous KIT-6 was then used as a hard template for the preparation of mesoporous LaCoO3 oxides. The typical synthesis procedure is as following: 1.3g lanthanum nitrate (La(NO3)3 nH2O) and 1.16g cobaltous nitrate (Co(NO3)2 6H2O) were dissolved in 20mL solvent (ethanol: deionized water=3: 1). Citric acid was added into the nitrate precursors as the chelating agent, and the molar ratio of the citric acid and metal ions was 1:1. The solution was incorporated into 0.5g as-prepared KIT-6 by the wetness impregnation technique. The solution is heating under stirring constantly to achieve a viscous mixture and then transfers into the oven to obtain a dried powder. The lanthanum and cobaltous precursors@silica composite was placed in oven and calcinated at 270°C for 2h firstly and then at 700°C under atmosphere. The silica template was finally removed by treating in 2M NaOH solution for three times. This template-free oxide was collected by centrifugation and washed with distilled water and ethanol, and then dried at 80°C. The synthesized samples were named as La2O3/Co3O4-t and LaCoO3-t (t =4, 6, 8), according to the different holidng time (4, 6, and 8h), respectively.

name: FeCu5
synthesis_para: Mesoporous CuFe2O4 solid solutions with a high specific surface area and different Cu/Fe molar ratios were synthesized by a novel and simple sol-gel route using FeCl3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·3H2O as Fe and Cu precursors, respectivily, and propylene oxide as gelation agent. In a typical synthesis, 10 mmol of FeCl3·6H2O with a calculated amount of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were dissolved in the appropriate amount of distilled water at room temperature. After stirring for 15 min, an amount of propylene oxide (propylene oxide/(Fe+Cu) molar ratio = 11) was added to the solution. The gel was formed within 5 min. The resulting gel was aged at room temperature for 1 h and then dried in air at 80 °C for 48 h, and finally calcined at 300 °C with a ramp rate of 3 °C/min for 5 h in an air atmosphere. The nominal contents of CuO were 5, 10, 15, 25, 30, and 40 mol%, and the corresponding catalysts were denoted as FeCu5, FeCu10, FeCu15, FeCu25, FeCu30, and FeCu40, respectively.

name: Meso-CeO2
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The various meso-MO x materials such as CeO2, Mn2O3, Co3O4, SnO2 and TiO2 were synthesized by the nano-replication method. Typically, the KIT-6 template was heated at 373K for 1h. Then, each precursor (Ce(NO3)·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·6H2O, CO(NO3)2·6H2O, SnCl2·6H2O, and Ti(OCH(CH3)2)4) was dissolved in distilled water, and impregnated into the silica template (see Table S1 about the detailed amounts of each precursor solution). Subsequently, the composites were put in an oven at 80°C overnight for the spontaneous infiltration of the precursor into the mesopores of the silica template. The each composite was heated at 450°C, 550°C, 550°C, 700°C and 450°C, respectively, under static air conditions for 3h. After the heat treatment, the silica template was removed using 2M NaOH solution 2 times. Finally, the meso-MO x materials thus obtained were washed with distilled water and acetone several times and dried at room temperature.

name: CeO2
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: Nanosized copper cerium bimetal oxides were prepared by a hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 1.6mmol of copper (II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O) and 6.4mmol of cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) were dissolved in 20mL of deionized water. In all cases, the concentrations of the total metal ions (i.e., [Cu(II)]+[Ce(III)]) were kept constant at 0.4M. 14.4mmol of NaOH solution (0.15M) was added into the above solution under vigorous stirring. After stirring for 10min, the obtained precipitate was collected by centrifugation and rinsed with distilled water for removing the excess metal ions by vacuum filtration. Then it was dispersed into 80mL deionized water and transferred into a 100mL PTFE autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was placed in an oven and maintained at 120°C for 5h. After the autoclave was cooled to ambient temperature naturally, the precipitate was dried at 80°C for 12h and then calcined at 500°C for 3h in air. The product was named as Ce0.80Cu0.20O2. For comparison, the percent of Cu doping content was varied from 0 to 25mol% and the calcination temperature was also varied from 300 to 600°C.

name: MnO2
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: Nanosized Ag doped MnO2 catalysts of the type Mn1−X Ag X O2 (where x =0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15 and 0.20) were prepared by co-precipitation and drying method. Stoichiometric amount of manganese acetate and silver nitrate were dissolved in distilled water. Both this solutions were added to 200ml distilled water at 100°C with stirring. Liquid ammonia solution was added till pH=9. Subsequently, the suspension was subjected to oxidation by drop wise addition of 30% H2O2 solution with constant stirring [15]. The suspension was then stirred continuously at 100°C till dryness. The resulting product was dried at 150°C for 6h and calcined in air at 400°C for 10h.

name: Mn0.98Ag0.02O2
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: Nanosized Ag doped MnO2 catalysts of the type Mn1−X Ag X O2 (where x =0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15 and 0.20) were prepared by co-precipitation and drying method. Stoichiometric amount of manganese acetate and silver nitrate were dissolved in distilled water. Both this solutions were added to 200ml distilled water at 100°C with stirring. Liquid ammonia solution was added till pH=9. Subsequently, the suspension was subjected to oxidation by drop wise addition of 30% H2O2 solution with constant stirring [15]. The suspension was then stirred continuously at 100°C till dryness. The resulting product was dried at 150°C for 6h and calcined in air at 400°C for 10h.

name: Co3O4-300-KIT-6-40
synthesis_para: Synthesis of mesoporous Co3O4 was performed by the “bi-solvent” impregnation method reported previously by Davidson and co-workers [37] Typically, 1.0g of as-prepared KIT-6 was suspended in 40mL of dry n-hexane. After stirring for 3h at room temperature, 1mL of 1.5g/mL Co(NO3)2·6H2O aqueous solution as the second solution was added dropwise under vigorous stirring. The mixture was stirred overnight. Then, a pink powder specimen was obtained by filtration and dried at room temperature. The solid was then calcined in a muffle furnace with a heating rate of 1°Cmin−1 from room temperature to 300°C and maintained at this temperature for 5h. The silica template was removed by etching twice with heated 2M NaOH aqueous solution for 12h each time. The template-free mesoporous Co3O4 was collected by filtering, washing with water and ethanol, and then dried at 80°C. Mesoporous Co3O4 prepared by using KIT-6-40 and KIT-6-130 was denoted as Co3O4-300-KIT-6-40 and Co3O4-300-KIT-6-130, respectively. In addition, in order to investigate the influence of the calcination temperature of the precursor Co(NO3)2·6H2O on catalytic activity, another two specimens designated as Co3O4-500-KIT-6-40 and Co3O4-500-KIT-6-130 were prepared following the same procedure described above except for the calcination temperature of 500°C.

name: MnO2
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: The solid catalysts of composition Mn1− X Pd X O2 (where X =0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.08) were prepared by dextrose aided co-precipitation technique. All reagents used were of analytical grade. Appropriate amount of manganese (II) acetate was dissolved in distilled water at room temperature, calculated amount of palladium (II) chloride was taken in a separate beaker, to this distilled water and concentrated nitric acid was added and heated with stirring to dissolve completely. Both these solutions were mixed together at room temperature to get a clear homogeneous solution. This solution was added to 2% dextrose solution at 100°C with stirring, dextrose solution was added to avoid agglomeration. The precipitation was carried out by adding drop by drop solution of sodium hydroxide (10%) to the above solution under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, the suspension of precipitated metal hydroxide mixture (at pH 9) was subjected to oxidation by drop wise addition of 30% H2O2 solution with constant stirring in order to adjust the appropriate oxidation state of the metal ions. The precipitate was then digested on a water bath for three hours, filtered and dried in air at 120°C for 10h. Finally the dried precipitate was homogenized well in mortar and calcined in air at 400°C for 5h.

name: FeCu10
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: All chemicals were used as received without further purification. In a typical synthesis procedure of the mesoporous CuO–Fe2O3 composite catalysts with different CuO content, 6mmol cetyltrimethylammonium bromide was dissolved into 200ml distilled water at room temperature, followed by ultrasound irradiation 15min. Then 10mmol Fe(NO3)3·6H2O and calculated amount of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were added into the above solution under magnetic stirring. After stirring for 0.5h, 0.2mol/l sodium hydroxide solution was gradually added until the pH value of the mixed solution was 9. The final suspended solution was aged at 90°C for 3h. The precipitate was filtered, washed with hot water, dried in the oven at 110°C for 12h, then milled and calcined at 300°C for 5h. The nominal content of CuO was 10, 15, 20, 25, 33 and 50mol%, and the corresponding catalysts were denoted as FeCu10, FeCu15, FeCu20, FeCu25, FeCu33 and FeCu50, respectively.

name: Co0.1MnOx
synthesis_para: Transition-metal-doped MnO2 nanowires were synthesized using a modified hydrothermal method [24]. In brief, 40 mL of aqueous solution containing 0.8 mmol KMnO4, 0.8 mmol NH4Cl, and 0.08 mmol transition metal nitrate (Fe, Co, Ni, and Cu, separately) was put into a 45 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The hydrothermal synthesis was conducted at 200 °C for 48 h in an electric oven. Afterward, the brown cottonlike product was collected by filtration and fully rinsed several times with deionized water to remove K+, followed by drying overnight at 120 °C. The obtained samples were denoted as Fe0.1MnOx, Co0.1MnOx, Ni0.1MnOx, and Cu0.1MnOx, respectively. In addition, Cu-doped MnO2 with different doping amounts (0.04 mmol and 0.16 mmol Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were used) was synthesized by the same method and denoted as Cu0.05MnOx and Cu0.2MnOx, respectively. Pure MnO2 nanowires without doping were synthesized as a reference under the same conditions and denoted as MnO2.

synthesis_para: Transition-metal-doped MnO2 nanowires were synthesized using a modified hydrothermal method [24]. In brief, 40 mL of aqueous solution containing 0.8 mmol KMnO4, 0.8 mmol NH4Cl, and 0.08 mmol transition metal nitrate (Fe, Co, Ni, and Cu, separately) was put into a 45 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The hydrothermal synthesis was conducted at 200 °C for 48 h in an electric oven. Afterward, the brown cottonlike product was collected by filtration and fully rinsed several times with deionized water to remove K+, followed by drying overnight at 120 °C. The obtained samples were denoted as Fe0.1MnOx, Co0.1MnOx, Ni0.1MnOx, and Cu0.1MnOx, respectively. In addition, Cu-doped MnO2 with different doping amounts (0.04 mmol and 0.16 mmol Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were used) was synthesized by the same method and denoted as Cu0.05MnOx and Cu0.2MnOx, respectively. Pure MnO2 nanowires without doping were synthesized as a reference under the same conditions and denoted as MnO2.

name: M8C1
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: The catalysts used in this study were prepared using a co-precipitation method. Mn(NO3)2 and Ce(NO3)3·6H2O were weighed according to different Mn and Ce molar ratios in the experiment. The reagents were added to deionized water and stirred. After complete dissolution, a 1 mol/L NaOH solution was added dropwise until the pH reached 9–10. After stirring the mixed solution for 40 min, it was allowed to stand for 24 h. The hybrid solution was subsequently filtered. During the filtration process, deionized water was used to wash the filtrate until the pH of the filtrate reached 7. The filtered product was dried at 110 °C for 12 h and calcined in an air atmosphere, resulting in manganese-cerium oxide catalysts. The catalysts were named based on different molar ratios of Mn and Ce. For example, M8C1 represents an Mn to Ce molar ratio of 8:1. Different calcination temperatures and times were applied to M8C1 to obtain the optimal parameters. The catalysts with different molar ratios were all calcined at 400 °C for 6 h.

name: NiCo2Ox
flow_normalisation_note: GHSV given (h^-1) — need bed volume per gram to convert
synthesis_para: Catalysts of different element-added Ni-Co composite oxides were prepared via the co-precipitation method: Ni:Co:X = 1:2:1 molar ratio (X is Mg, Fe, Cu, Ce, respectively) of C4H6NiO4·4H2O, C4H6CoO4·4H2O, and nitric acid X aqueous compounds were weighed and dissolved in 300 mL of deionized water to form solution A, and then placed into an ultrasonic cleaner for 30 min, equipped with anhydrous sodium carbonate solution B and sodium hydroxide solution C (2 mol/L) according to the same molar ratio (the sum of ionic concentrations of Ni and Co). Solution A was added drop by drop into solution B under the condition of water bath at 60 °C, and stirred with a magnetic stirrer at 600 r/min. During the process, Solution C was used to adjust the pH, and the pH of the solution was maintained at about 10 by controlling the amount of dropwise addition. At the end of the reaction, the stirring continued for 1 h, and the precipitate obtained was aged at 60 °C for 18 h. The precipitate was washed by filtration with deionized water until the filtrate was neutral. The obtained product was then heated and dried in a drying oven at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling, the product was roasted in a muffle furnace at a heating rate of 5 °C/min to 600 °C for 6 h. Finally, different element-added NiCo2Ox composite oxides composite oxide catalysts were obtained, which were named Mg/NiCo2Ox, Fe/NiCo2Ox, Cu/NiCo2Ox, and Ce/NiCo2Ox, respectively

name: AgCo
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: Silver–cobalt and silver–manganese (1 : 1 molar ratio) composite oxide catalysts were prepared by co-precipitation technique. AgNO3 (Aldrich, 99.8%), Co(NO3)2 · 6H2O (Fluka, 99.0%) and Mn(NO3)2 · 4H2O (Riedel, 97.0%) at desired molar ratios were dissolved in distilled water. The total concentration of the metals in the combined aqueous solution was 0:1 M. Na2CO3 (1 M) solution was put into the stirrer. 0:1 M of silver–cobalt (or silver–manganese) solution was added to the sodiumcarbonate solution to make the pH of the solution 8. The precipitates were aged for 3 h at pH 8 and ?ltered and washed with hot distilled water several times for removing excess ions. They were dried in air at 110◦C overnight. Catalysts were calcined at 200◦C and 500◦C for 3 h.

name: CeO2-CuOx
synthesis_para: The CeO2-MOx (M=Cu, Mn, Fe, Co, and Ni) materials were prepared by a citric acid complexation-combustion method[14]. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·2H2O (or Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·6H2O and Ni(NO3)2·9H2O) were dissolved in de-ionized water with M/Ce=1/9 (molar ratio). Citric acid (Citric acid/M=1.2) was added in the nitrate salts aqueous solution. The synthesis solution was stirred at 70 °C till this solution was changed into gel. Once the gel formed, this gel was quickly elevated to 150 °C, resulting in the gel foam and sparks formation. After the sparks were extinguished, a solid product was obtained. Then the obtained powder was calcined at 600 °C for 4 h in air and ground into 40–60 mesh.

name: CeO2-CuOx
synthesis_para: The CeO2-MOx (M=Cu, Mn, Fe, Co, and Ni) materials were prepared by a citric acid complexation-combustion method[14]. Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Cu(NO3)2·2H2O (or Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·6H2O and Ni(NO3)2·9H2O) were dissolved in de-ionized water with M/Ce=1/9 (molar ratio). Citric acid (Citric acid/M=1.2) was added in the nitrate salts aqueous solution. The synthesis solution was stirred at 70 °C till this solution was changed into gel. Once the gel formed, this gel was quickly elevated to 150 °C, resulting in the gel foam and sparks formation. After the sparks were extinguished, a solid product was obtained. Then the obtained powder was calcined at 600 °C for 4 h in air and ground into 40–60 mesh.

name: Cu0.09C0.91eOx
synthesis_para: In a typical synthetic procedure, 2–6 mmol of ammonium cerium(IV) nitrate and an appropriate amount of the acetylacetonate salts of the dopant metal (Cu, Sm, and Co) were dissolved in 20 mL of oleyl amine. The reaction mixture was heated up to 100 °C under gentle magnetic stirring until the formation of a clear solution. Subsequently, the reaction temperature was raised to 300 °C and remained at this temperature for 1 h. Thereafter, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and the nanoparticles were precipitated by the addition of a large excess of ethanol. The precipitate was isolated by centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min. To remove the excess unbonded oleyl amine molecules and other reaction byproducts, the particles were redissolved in chloroform, precipitated by ethanol, and isolated by centrifugation. The above procedure was repeated three times. Finally, the surface-bonded oleyl amine molecules were removed by a calcination step at 450 °C for 2 h under a continuous air flow. The methodology is characterized by simplicity, repeatability, and the ability to synthesize monodispersed, up to a 1 g scale, nanoparticle quantities.

name: Co0.98Sn0.02
synthesis_para: A series of Sn-modified Co3O4 catalysts were prepared by co-precipitation method with the same procedure as Sm-modified Co3O4 catalysts described formerly [24]. In detail, 1 mol L−1 K2CO3 aqueous solution was dripped slowly into the mixed solutions of Co(NO3)2 (0.5 M) and SnCl4 solution (0.5 M) with desired Co/Sn molar ratios until the pH reached 9. Afterwards, the mixture was stirred for 1 h and aged for 3 h before vacuum filtering. The precipitate was then washed thoroughly with deionized water until Cl−1 free (detected by 0.01 M AgNO3 solution) and its total dissolved solids were less than 20 ppm. The obtained solids were dried in air at 110 °C overnight. The dried precipitate was then calcined at 300 °C for 6 h in air atmosphere to get the final catalyst for activity test and regular characterization. For phase identification, the catalysts were also calcined at 600 °C to get better crystallization. The obtained Co–Sn binary catalysts were denoted by Co1−xSnx, in which x indicates the molar ratio of Sn/(Co + Sn). In addition, pure Co3O4 and SnO2 samples were prepared by the identical procedure for the comparison purpose.

name: SnO2
synthesis_para: Mesoporous Cu–Sn mixed oxides with different Cu/Sn atomic ratios were prepared by an easy co-precipitation method. In detail, the desired amount of SnCl4 (0.5 mol L−1) and Cu(NO3)2 (0.5 mol L−1) solution was mixed and stirred thoroughly at room temperature for 60 min. Then Na2CO3 solution (0.5 mol L−1) was dripped slowly into the above solution mixture until the pH reached 8, which was followed by another 60 min's continuous stirring. Afterwards, the precipitate was vacuum-filtered and washed with distilled deionized (DDI) water until Cl− free, with a total dissolved solid (TDS) less than 10 ppm. The precipitate was dried at 110 °C overnight, and then calcined at 300 °C in air atmosphere for 4 h. For comparison study, pure SnO2 and CuO were prepared by the same method.

name: NiCeOx
flow_normalisation_note: insufficient data (need mL/h/g, or mL/min + mass, or WHSV with units)
synthesis_para: The microwave-accelerated reaction system (MARS-6) for material synthesis was used in this study and has been described elsewhere. (18) The doped-ceria materials were prepared by dissolving the precursor salts Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and M(NO3)3·xH2O (M = metal heteroatom) in distilled water with the respective molar ratios for obtaining the final composition of M–Ce–O, where M: Fe, Mn, Ni, Co, Zn, Cu, and Zr and nominal M/Ce ratio: 0.25. Citric acid was used as a complexing agent. Calcination in static air, ca. 500 °C for 6 h under atmospheric pressure, was performed in all samples. More details on the applied synthesis and calcination procedures applied are provided in the Supporting Information.

(18) The doped-ceria materials were prepared by dissolving the precursor salts Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and M(NO3)3·xH2O (M = metal heteroatom) in distilled water with the respective molar ratios for obtaining the final composition of M–Ce–O, where M: Fe, Mn, Ni, Co, Zn, Cu, and Zr and nominal M/Ce ratio: 0.25. Citric acid was used as a complexing agent. Calcination in static air, ca. 500 °C for 6 h under atmospheric pressure, was performed in all samples. More details on the applied synthesis and calcination procedures applied are provided in the Supporting Information.

name: MnaCobOx
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The method for preparing polymer nanofilm-coated MnaCobOx catalysts was modified from the solid-phase method in our previous works. (25,26) Briefly, to increase the homogeneity and interactions between the added components, aqueous solution of metal nitrites instead of their solid forms were added to the polymer precursors oxalic acid (OA, ≥ 99.0%) and ethylene glycol (EG, ≥ 99.0%) which were also in liquid form and mixed well in advance. In a typical synthesis procedure, solid Co(NO3)2·6H2O (≥99.0%) was first dissolved in the aqueous solution of Mn(NO3)2 (50 wt %), and then added with the mixture of OA and EG (EG/OA = 1:1, mol) at a calculated ratio. The mixed solution was ground in an agate mortar for 30 min, then transferred into a glass cup. After being ultrasonicated for 1 h, the sample was dried at 110 °C overnight and then calcined at 230 °C for 4 h. The whole synthesis procedure is schematically illustrated in Scheme 1. The catalysts are denoted as MnaCobOx.

name: CF8-1
synthesis_para: In a typical preparation procedure: Fe(SO4)2·7H2O and Co(NO3)2·6H2O were mixed together according to the mole ratio and dissolved in 100 ml water. Then, sodium oxalate solution was slowly added. After stirring for 2 hours, the precipitate was filtered, washed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C overnight. The x/y stands for the Fe/Co mole ratio in the parent solution. A series of mesoporous Fe-doped cobalt spinel oxides were obtained by calcinating the precursors Fex/(x+y)Coy/(x+y)C2O4·6H2O at 250 °C for 2 h. The heating rate during calcination should be controlled to be as low as 1 °C min−1.

name: CeZrCu10
synthesis_para: The CuO/Ce0.8Zr0.2O2 catalysts with different CuO content were synthesized using a surfactant-assisted method of nanoparticle assembly. At room temperature, 6 mmol of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was dissolved into 200 ml distilled water under ultrasound irradiation for 15 min, then 8 mmol of Ce(NO3)3·6H2O, 2 mmol of Zr(NO3)4·5H2O and calculated amount of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O were added under vigorous stirring. After stirring for 0.5 h, 0.2 mol/l sodium hydroxide solution was gradually added into the above solution until the pH value of the mixed solution was 10, and then further stirred for about 12 h. The final suspended solution was aged at 90 °C for 3 h, washed with hot water, dried in the oven at 110 °C for 6 h, then milled and calcined at 400 °C for 4 h. The content of CuO was 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40 mol%, and the corresponding catalysts were denoted as CeZrCu0, CeZrCu5, CeZrCu10, CeZrCu15, CeZrCu20, CeZrCu25, CeZrCu30, CeZrCu40, respectively. In order to make clear the influence of the calcination temperature on the catalyst property, a series of CeZrCu25 catalysts calcined at different temperature were prepared in the similar manner.
description: mesoporous nanocrystalline particle particles nanoscaled mesoporous nanostructured

name: SnO2-CuMnOx-A
synthesis_para: In this study, to imitate commercialized Hopcalite catalysts, the target formulation with 33 wt.% Cu and 67 wt.% Mn element contents were used, which is a typical composition of Hopcalite catalysts. To investigate the modification effects of SnO2 on Hopcalite catalysts, a series of Cu-Mn-Sn-O catalysts with a fixed Cu content, but with part of the Mn element replaced by Sn element, were prepared. All the chemicals used in this study for sample preparation are analytical (A.R.) grade without further purification, which were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. An aqueous solution mixture of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (15 mL, 0.58 mol L−1), Mn(NO3)2 (aq, 50 wt.%) and SnCl4·5H2O (0.16 mol L−1) was added dropwise into a Na2CO3 aqueous solution (20 mL, 2.5 mol L−1) maintained at 70 °C in a water bath under vigorous stirring. Afterwards, nitric acid solution (2 mol L−1) was added dropwise into the above solution mixture until the pH reached ∼8.3, which was stirred continuously for another 4 h. The precipitate was then vacuum-filtered and washed with DDI water thoroughly until Cl− free, with a total dissolved solid (TDS) below 20 ppm. The resulted precipitate was dried at 110 °C overnight, calcined at 400 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in air atmosphere for 4 h and then grounded into powder. Four catalysts with different amount of SnO2, which was confirmed by ICP to be 2.7 wt.%, 6.5 wt.%, 8.5 wt.% and 10.6 wt.% Sn element (see details in Table S1), were synthesized. Accordingly, the catalysts were named as SnO2-CuMnOx-A, SnO2-CuMnOx-B, SnO2-CuMnOx-C and SnO2-CuMnOx-D, respectively. For comparison study, a self-prepared un-modified CuMnOx Hopcalite and a commercialized Hopcalite catalyst were also investigated.

name: Co/Fe = 1/0
synthesis_para: Mixed oxides containing iron and cobalt catalysts were provided by co-precipitation method. In a typical preparation, firstly, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (Merck, 98%) and Co(NO3)2·6H2O (Merck, 99%) with different molar ratios were dissolved in distillated water and the resulting solution was heated to 70 °C. Then an aqueous solution of sodium carbonate (0.5 M) was added to mixed nitrate solution under continuous stirring at a rate of 2–3 ml/min at 70 °C until pH 7.0 was achieved. The resulting precipitate was aged at pH of 7.0 for 2 h and then filtered and washed with warm distilled water several times for removing excess ions. Afterward, the precipitate was dried in air at 110 °C for 16 h and finally calcined at 400 °C in air atmosphere for 6 h with a ramp rate of 3 °C/min.

name: Ni0.8Co0.2
synthesis_para: The Ni–Co composite oxides and the NiO and Co3O4 were prepared by the liquid-precipitation method without any surfactant. Briefly, an appropriate amount of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and CO(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in deionized water to obtain 2 mol L−1 Ni(NO3)2 and CO(NO3)2 aqueous solutions. The two aqueous solutions were mixed with constant stirring at an ambient temperature to obtain mixed aqueous solutions of different Ni/Co molar ratios (theoretical ratios Ni/Co = 99 : 1, 95 : 1, 9 : 1, 8 : 2, 7 : 3). Subsequently, excess diluted ammonia was added to these aqueous solutions dropwise (also with vigorous stirring) until the pH reached ∼10, and suspensions were obtained. After further stirring for 4 h and the samples were left to mature for 18 h at room temperature, before the products were collected by centrifugation. The products were washed consecutively with deionized water and absolute alcohol three times, in sequence, before being dried at 80 °C for 12 h. The obtained presoma were grinded fully and calcined in a muffle furnace at 400 °C for 4 h. For simplification, the samples are denoted as Ni1−xCox, for instance, the sample with a theoretical ratio Ni/Co = 99 : 1 is denoted as Ni0.99Co0.1. For comparison, pure NiO, Co3O4 and other Ni0.8M0.2 (where M = Mn, Fe, Zn, Cr) oxides were prepared using the same procedure.
description: mesoporous composite as-prepared amorphous solid solution plate - like flower - like granules

name: La0.7-Sr0.3-Co-O
synthesis_para: The samples were prepared by a sol-gel method using ethylene glycol and formic acid as complexing agents. Briefly, 5 mmol of La(NO3)3 and/or Sr(NO3)2, and 5 mmol of Co(NO3)3, Fe(NO3)3, Ni(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2 or Zn(NO3)2 were first dissolved in a mixed solution containing 3 mL ethylene glycol and 2 mL formic acid and stirred for 6 h. The resulting solution was then aged at 95 °C overnight, yielding a gel-like product, which was calcined in a static air oven at 600 °C for 5 h (heating rate 1 °C min-1). Such obtained samples showed a multi-phase structure and thus were denoted as La1-x-Srx-B-O (B = Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, 0 ≤ x ≤ 0.7). For comparison, a perovskite La0.7Sr0.3CoO3 was prepared by calcination at 800 °C, and a metal oxide Co3O4 was prepared without the addition of La or Sr, with otherwise identical conditions.

name: La-Co-O
synthesis_para: The samples were prepared by a sol-gel method using ethylene glycol and formic acid as complexing agents.
