name: Bulk LaCoO3
synthesis_para: For comparison purposes, a bulk LaCoO3 catalyst was prepared by the citric acid-complexing method described elsewhere [23]. The obtained solid precursor was first calcined in air at a ramp of 1 °C/min in a muffle furnace from RT to 300 °C and maintained at this temperature for 3 h, and then from 300 to 850 °C at the ramp of 5 °C/min and kept at 850 °C for 3 h.

name: 3DOM LaCoO3
synthesis_para: The 3DOM LaCoO3 catalysts were prepared using the well-arrayed PMMA microspheres (with an average diameter of ca. 300 nm) as hard template [22]. In a typical procedure, 8.66 g of La(NO3)3·6H2O and 5.821 g of Co(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in 7.0 mL of methanol and 3.0 mL of ethylene glycol (EG) at room temperature (RT) under stirring for 2 h, obtaining a transparent solution. 0.7 g of l-lysine was dissolved in 2.0 mL of deionized water and the pH value of the solution was adjusted to around 5 with a certain amount of citric acid. Then, the l-lysine-containing solution and the metal nitrate-containing transparent solution were mixed together under stirring for 1 h, obtaining the precursor solution. 2.0 g of the PMMA hard template was thoroughly soaked in the precursor solution for 4 h. After filtration and drying, the solid was transferred to a ceramic boat and thermally treated in a tubular furnace first in a N2 flow of 50 mL/min at a ramp of 1 °C/min from RT to 300 °C for 3 h, then cooled in the same atmosphere to RT, and finally in an air flow of 50 mL/min at a ramp of 1 °C/min from RT to 650 °C and kept at this temperature for 3 h.

name: Co3O4
synthesis_para: Bulk Co3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized by coprecipitation. Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.25 mmol, Sigma-Aldrich) was added in 25 mL of deionized (DI) water. Under vigorous stirring, 25 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (NaOH, 0.375 M) was instilled dropwise into the initial precursor solution and then the solution was stirred for 2 h. The precipitate was filtered with DI water and ethyl alcohol. After drying at 50 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven, the resulting cakes were crushed into fine powder in a mortar. The powders were calcined in static air at 500 °C for 5 h. La-doped Co3O4 catalysts were synthesized using the same procedure except that lanthanum nitrate hexahydrate (La(NO3)3·6H2O, Sigma-Aldrich) was added in 25 mL of DI water with an adjusted molar concentration (La = 10, 20, and 30 mol %). LaCoO3 perovskite was synthesized using the same method with a Co/La molar ratio of 1:1. La2O3 nanoparticles were synthesized for characterizations using the same method without Co precursor.

name: CeSnO–0.9
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: A set of CeXSn1−XO2−δ mixed oxides (X ranging from 0.975 to 0.80) were prepared by co-precipitation method, from cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) and tin chloride (SnCl2·2H2O) as precursors. The resultant solids were repeatedly washed, dried and calcined at 500 °C for 5 h in air. The synthesis of the mixed oxides was also carried out at pH = 9 and room temperature, baking the resultant suspension for 1 h. The resultant slurry was filtered and washed several times with deionized water, dried overnight at 115 °C and calcined in air at 500 °C for 5 h. The obtained solids were denoted as CeSnO–X, being X the mol fraction of ceria in the mixed oxide.

name: CeSnO–0.85
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: A set of CeXSn1−XO2−δ mixed oxides (X ranging from 0.975 to 0.80) were prepared by co-precipitation method, from cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O) and tin chloride (SnCl2·2H2O) as precursors. The resultant solids were repeatedly washed, dried and calcined at 500 °C for 5 h in air. The synthesis of the mixed oxides was also carried out at pH = 9 and room temperature, baking the resultant suspension for 1 h. The resultant slurry was filtered and washed several times with deionized water, dried overnight at 115 °C and calcined in air at 500 °C for 5 h. The obtained solids were denoted as CeSnO–X, being X the mol fraction of ceria in the mixed oxide.

name: LaCo0.5Fe0.5O3
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The LaCo0.5M0.5O3 (M = Mn, Cr, Fe, Ni, Cu) perovskite oxides catalysts were prepared by the citrate method. A concentrated solution of the metal nitrates was mixed with an aqueous solution of citric acid by fixing at unity the molar ratio of citric acid to the metal cations. Water was evaporated under slow stirring from the mixed solution at 90 °C with aqueous bath in the oven until a viscous gel was obtained. The gel was then dried at 100 °C overnight and calcined at 800 °C under atmosphere for 5 h.

name: Co3O4
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: To synthesize the mesoporous Co3O4, 5 g (0.017 mol) of Co(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in a solution containing 17 g (0.33 mol) of 1-butanol, 2.4 g (0.038 mol) of HNO3, and 2.5 g (4.31 × 10–4 mol) of P123 in a 400 mL beaker. After magnetic stirring at room temperature, a clear gel was formed. The clear gel was then placed in an oven at 120 °C for 3.5 h. The obtained powder was washed with ethanol several times, centrifuged, and dried in a vacuum oven. The powders were calcined at 150 °C for 12 h (designated UCT-7) and further heated to 250 °C, 350 °C, and 450 °C, respectively, for 1 h with a heating rate of 1 °C/min (designated UCT-8). All the heating cycles were done under air. Samples were nominated as Meso-Co-X (X = 150, 250, 350, and 450), representing samples heat treated at 150 °C, 250 °C, 350 °C, and 450 °C, respectively. Commercial Co3O4 (C–Co3O4) from Strem Chemical Inc. was 99.5% in purity and used as received.

name: Ce0.90Co0.10O2–δ
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The nanocrystalline solid solution catalyst, Ce0.90Co0.10O2–δ, was prepared using a single-step combustion method [12]. All the metal precursors and chemicals used in the preparation of the catalysts were procured from Sigma-Aldrich with purity 99.9%. In the preparation, a redox combustion mixture was composed of (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6, Co(NO3)2.5H2O and NH2CONH2 in the ratio of 0.90: 0.1: 3.77 respectively. Typically, a mixture of 9.869 g of (NH4)2Ce(NO3)6, 0.582 g of Co(NO3)2.5H2O and 4.529 g of NH2CONH2 was dissolved in 50 cm3 of water in a borosilicate dish. The resulting solution was heated at 150 °C on a hotplate for 10 min. The boiling solution was then transferred into a muffle furnace, preheated to 400 °C. After almost complete dehydration, the slurry ignited, resulting in the formation of a brown solid product. A similar one-step combustion method was used to prepare the Ce0.90-xCuxCo0.10O2–δ (where x = 0.01, 0.03 and 0.05) bimetallic solid solutions, using Cu(NO3)2.3H2O as a precursor, in addition to the other starting materials mentioned in the preparation of Ce0.90Co0.10O2–δ.

name: Mn2O3
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: Pd doped Mn2O3 catalysts having the composition Mn2−XPdXO3 (where X = 0, 0.05 and 0.08) were prepared by a dextrose aided co-precipitation method. Appropriate amounts of manganese(II) acetate and palladium(II) chloride were dissolved in distilled water. Both these solutions were added to 2 % dextrose solution at 100 °C with stirring, dextrose solution was added to avoid agglomeration of the particles. The precipitation was carried out by adding a solution of sodium hydroxide (10 %) dropwise to the above solution under vigorous stirring. Subsequently the suspension of precipitated metal hydroxide mixture (at pH 9) was subjected to oxidation by the dropwise addition of 30 % H2O2 solution with constant stirring in order to adjust the appropriate oxidation state of metal ions. The precipitate was then filtered, dried at 120 °C for 5 h and calcined at 700 °C for 5 h. Pristine Mn2O3 was also prepared by similar method without addition of palladium chloride.

name: Mn3Co16Ox-S
synthesis_para: The MnCoOx composite oxides were prepared by a solid-phase method using different manganese salt precursors, namely manganese nitrate (Mn(NO3)3), manganese acetate (Mn(CH3COO)2) and manganese sulfate (MnSO4), respectively. Taking the catalyst using Mn(NO3)2 precursor as an example, stoichiometric amounts of cobalt nitrate (Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Sinopharm, AR), Mn(NO3)3·9H2O (Sinopharm, AR), oxalic acid (OA, Sinopharm, AR) and ethylene glycol (EG, Sinopharm, AR) were weighed and placed in an agate mortar, and the molar ratio of Mn/Co and OA/EG was 3/16 and 1/1, respectively. After grinding for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous mixture, the resultant solid was transferred into a glass beaker and underwent sonication for 1 h. After drying at 110 °C overnight and calcination at 230 °C for 4 h under an air atmosphere, the MnCoOx composite oxides were finally yielded and simply designated as Mn3Co16Ox-N. The MnCoOx catalysts using manganese acetate and manganese sulfate as the manganese precursors followed the same preparation procedures, and were namely designated as Mn3Co16Ox-A and Mn3Co16Ox-S, respectively.

name: Mn3Co16Ox-N
synthesis_para: The MnCoOx composite oxides were prepared by a solid-phase method using different manganese salt precursors, namely manganese nitrate (Mn(NO3)3), manganese acetate (Mn(CH3COO)2) and manganese sulfate (MnSO4), respectively. name: CuMnOx
synthesis_para: The copper manganese oxide catalysts were prepared by co-precipitation using NH4HCO3 as the precipitant, the acetates of the copper and manganese as the precursors, and Fe(NO3)3, Ce(NO3)3, and Zn(NO3)2 as dopant. The typical procedure to synthesize the catalysts was as follows. A precipitant (30 mmol) was dissolved in deionized water (30 mL) with an initial pH value of ca. 8. The precursors (7.5 mmol) were mixed with deionized water (30 mL) with a 1/2 molar ratio of copper to manganese species. The mixed precursor solution was then added to the precipitant solution at 298 K with vigorous stirring. The resultant suspensions were aged for 30 min with continued vigorous stirring at 25 °C. Finally the precipitate was filtered, washed with deionized water and anhydrous alcohol, dried in air at 50 °C for 24 h, and then calcination at 300 °C for 2 h (denoted as CuMOx-M, M = Fe, Zn, Ce) to obtain the final catalysts. The content of the doping transition metal oxide was fixed at 5 wt%. For comparison, the pure copper oxide and manganese oxide catalysts were prepared separately using the acetate as the precursor and NaOH as the precipitant, and keeping the other synthesis and after-treatment conditions the same as the copper manganese oxides. The corresponding samples were named CuO and MnOx, respectively.

name: 10-CC
synthesis_para: A series of CuO–CeO2 catalysts with different CuO contents were synthesized via a direct solvothermal method. Typically, specified amounts of Cu and Ce nitrates were dissolved in EG. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave of 80 mL capacity and heated at 180 °C for 3 h with continuous stirring. Then the obtained suspension was naturally cooled down to room temperature (RT). The products thus obtained were separated by filtration, then washed with deionized water and ethanol, dried at 120 °C overnight and calcined under static air at 450 °C (based on our previous study28) for 4 h. The CuO loadings of these catalysts were 0, 5, 10, 20, and 30 mol% expressed as the mole ratio of Cu/(Cu + Ce), which were denoted as CeO2, 5-CC, 10-CC, 20-CC, and 30-CC, respectively.

name: FeOx-MnOx
synthesis_para: For the preparation of MnCO3, 0.01 mol MnSO4·H2O and 0.01 mol NaHCO3 were separately dissolved in 700 mL deionized water. Then the NaHCO3 solution was added into the MnSO4 solution under stirring. The mixture was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. Finally, the suspension was filtered and washed with deionized water, followed by drying at 80 °C in an oven overnight. For the preparation of Fe0.2Mn0.8CO3, 0.008 mol MnSO4·H2O and 0.002 mol FeSO4·7H2O were dissolved to form a homogeneous solution. The following steps were same to those of the preparation of MnCO3. For the preparation of MnOx and FeOx-MnOx, 1 g of the as-prepared precursor of MnCO3 or Fe0.2Mn0.8CO3 was dispersed in 40 mL deionized water. Then 5 mL KMnO4 solution (0.032 mol L− 1) was added under stirring. After being reacted for 40 min, 5 mL HCl solution (2.4 mol L− 1) was added into the above solution, and the suspension was stirred for another 2 min. The precipitate was centrifuged and washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times and dried at 110 °C overnight. Finally, the products were calcined in a muffle furnace at 300 °C for 3 h in static air.

name: MnOx
synthesis_para: For the preparation of MnCO3, 0.01 mol MnSO4·H2O and 0.01 mol NaHCO3 were separately dissolved in 700 mL deionized water. Then the NaHCO3 solution was added into the MnSO4 solution under stirring. The mixture was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. Finally, the suspension was filtered and washed with deionized water, followed by drying at 80 °C in an oven overnight. For the preparation of Fe0.2Mn0.8CO3, 0.008 mol MnSO4·H2O and 0.002 mol FeSO4·7H2O were dissolved to form a homogeneous solution. The following steps were same to those of the preparation of MnCO3. For the preparation of MnOx and FeOx-MnOx, 1 g of the as-prepared precursor of MnCO3 or Fe0.2Mn0.8CO3 was dispersed in 40 mL deionized water. Then 5 mL KMnO4 solution (0.032 mol L− 1) was added under stirring. After being reacted for 40 min, 5 mL HCl solution (2.4 mol L− 1) was added into the above solution, and the suspension was stirred for another 2 min. The precipitate was centrifuged and washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times and dried at 110 °C overnight. Finally, the products were calcined in a muffle furnace at 300 °C for 3 h in static air.

name: Ce0.75Zr0.25
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: CeO2–ZrO2 mixed oxide catalysts were prepared by using the sol–gel technique. Cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (99.0%), Fluka), and zirconium oxychloride (ZrOCl2·8H2O (99.0%), Fluka) were used as sources of Ce and Zr, respectively. The starting solution was prepared by mixing 0.1 M of metal salt solutions with 0.4 M of urea solution at a 2 to 1 volumetric ratio. The ratio between each metal salt was altered depending on the desired solid solution concentration: Ce1−xZrxO2,where x=0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75 and 1.0. The solution was kept at 100°C for either 50 or 120 h. The sample was then allowed to cool to room temperature before being centrifuged to separate the solid product from the solution. The solid product was washed with ethanol to prevent an agglomeration of the particles, and then dried overnight in an oven at 110°C followed by calcination at either 500 or 900°C for 4 h in air.

name: CeO2−La2O3 773
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The CeO2−La2O3 (8:2 mol ratio based on oxides) solid solution was synthesized by a modified coprecipitation method from ultrahigh dilute solutions. This method is more attracting because of its relative simplicity, environmental friendliness, economical effectiveness, and suitability for large scale production. (29) Coprecipitation was carried out from Ce(NO3)3·6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade) and La(NO3)3·6H2O (Aldrich, AR grade) precursors, and a dilute aqueous ammonia solution was used as the precipitating agent. The requisite quantities of precursors were dissolved separately in double distilled water and mixed together under continuous stirring. The mixed solution was precipitated by dropwise addition of the aqueous ammonia solution over a period until the solution pH reached ∼8.5. The obtained light yellow colored slurry was decanted, filtered, and washed several times with doubly distilled water until free from anion impurities. The collected precipitate was oven-dried at 393 K for 12 h, crushed using an agate mortar, and subsequently calcined at 773 K for 5 h at a heating rate of 5 K min−1 in air atmosphere to obtain the mixed oxide material. Finally, some portions of the finished catalyst were further heated at 873, 973, and 1073 K for 5 h using the same heating ramp.

name: Fe2O3
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: Cobalt­iron composite oxides were prepared using the hydrothermal method. Cobalt(II) chloride (Wako Pure Chemicals Industry) and iron(III) chloride (Wako Pure Chemicals Industry) were dissolved in distilled water at room temperature under vigorous stirring, and then ethylene glycol (Wako Pure Chemicals Industry) was added to the solution. The solution thus obtained was transferred to a Teflon vessel, and then an aqueous solution of sodium(I) hydroxide (Wako Pure Chemicals Industry) was added to adjust the pH value to 13. The resulting wet gel was treated at 180°C for 4 h in an autoclave. The product thus obtained was washed with ethanol and distilled water, followed by drying and calcination at 500°C for 1 h in air. The Co content was changed from 10 to 70 atm% as a metal. The samples are abbreviated as Co(x)­Fe2O3, where x is the atomic concentration of Co. Iron oxide and cobalt oxide are expressed as Fe2O3 and Co3O4, respectively.

name: Co3O4
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: 4.98 g of cobalt acetate tetrahydrate was dissolved in 60 ml of ethylene glycol and the mixture was gradually heated to 160 °C. 200 ml of aqueous 0.2 M Na2CO3 solution was then added and the slurry was further aged for 1 h under vigorous stirring and a continuous flow of nitrogen. After filtration and being washed with water, the solid obtained was dried at 50 °C overnight under vacuum and calcined at 450 °C for 4 h in air.

name: CuMnCPRC
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The various methods applied in the synthesis of hopcalite catalysts are discussed below. In the Co-precipitation method, a solution of manganese acetate (14.70 g in 33 mL H2O) was added to 3.68 g of copper (II) nitrate and stirred for 1 h. After mixing, it was precipitated by (6.32 g in 33 mL H2O) KMnO4 solutions. All the chemicals uses are analytical reagent grade purchase from Forbes Pharmaceutical, Ghatkopar East, Mumbai, India. The precipitate was filtered and washed several times with warm distilled water to eliminate all the anions. After washing the precipitants was dried up to 110 °C temperature for 24 h followed reactive calcination in the presence of (4.5% CO in the atmosphere) at an entire flow rate of 32.5 mL min−1 in the warm precursors.

name: CuMnSGRC
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: In the sol-gel method, the manganese acetate and copper (II) nitrate salts were mixed in distilled water in stoichiometric amount then added citric acid as a complexing agent. The solution slowly changed into the lucent and viscous gel. The pH of solutions was maintained at 7.5–8.0 with the acetic acid, stirring and heating at a temperature of 80 °C for 4 h, then after the gel obtained heated to a temperature 110 °C for 24 h in the air, followed by reactive calcination in the presence of (4.5% CO in the atmosphere) at an entire flow rate of 32.5 mL min−1 in the warm precursors.

name: CuMnPRC
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: In the pyrolysis process, the Cu-nitrate and Mn-acetate was added into a distilled water at a stoichiometric ratio then fed throughout a commercial nozzle with liquid supply rates ranging from 2.5 to 5 mL/min dried by a lock supporting flame. The flame gas flow contains 1.5 mL/min CH4 and 3 mL/min O2. Thermal mass flow regulator monitored every gas flows and binder collects synthesis black capacious powders was obtained and underwent reactive calcination in the presence of (4.5% CO in the atmosphere) at an entire flow rate of 32.5 mL min−1 in the warm precursors.

name: CuMnSGRC
flow_normalisation_note: from volumetric flow and mass (mL/min ÷ g × 60)
synthesis_para: The CuMnOx catalysts sythesized by the reactive grinding method were following the two main steps. Initially, the Cu-nitrate and Mn acetate were mixed in stoichiometric ratio then added KMnO4 solutions in the zirconia jar of planetary ball mill. The mill was run at 150 rpm for 24 h. The sample obtained after reactive grinding was cleaned various times with distilled water and ethanol then dried in an oven at 110 °C for overnight followed by reactive calcination in the presence of (4.5% CO in the atmosphere) at an entire flow rate of 32.5 mL min−1 in the warm precursors.
